ground_truth.title: Teaching Online: Effective Practices
ground_truth.main_content:
This comprehensive guide addresses effective practices for teaching online. Online classes maintain the same rigor and expectations for students' time and effort as in-person courses, using evidence-based teaching strategies like backward design. However, online classes require additional time for design and building, need different approaches to communicating expectations, and introduce different ways of thinking about credit hours and seat time.

The guide covers three main areas:

1. Format, Rigor, and Integrity - Federal and state regulations require students to have regular and substantive academic interactions with the instructor initiated by the instructor. Students must have a required participation activity at least once each week. Learning outcomes and instruction time must be equivalent to in-person classes. The syllabus should clearly state any required synchronous sessions. Academic integrity policies specific to online learning should be included in the course syllabus.

2. Technology and Materials - All course materials must be accessible and meet copyright requirements. Documents should be in accessible formats for students with disabilities. Content should be organized into weekly modules using CarmenCanvas. The syllabus should list all required technology with support instructions, including accessibility, data privacy, and cost information for non-Ohio State technologies.

3. Teaching Strategies - Four areas significantly impact online student learning: instructor presence, student-student interaction, variety of teaching methods, and student support and metacognition. Strategies include regular instructor communications, peer interaction opportunities, varied learning activities, and guidance on learning goals and course design.
with: Online classes have the same rigor and expectations for students' time and effort, and the same week-by-week teaching pace and regular interaction with students.
Federal and state regulations require students to have regular and substantive academic interactions with the instructor that are initiated by the instructor.
All materials in your course must be accessible and meet copyright and fair use requirements.
Students are more engaged when they perceive their instructor as a frequent and positive presence in the course.
Student success is maximized when you provide frequent and varied learning activities and multiple means of demonstrating learning.
without: If you have a disability and experience difficulty accessing this content
